publications.editedComment: Frantic lines become a click record here. Every press increments the session count, changes the button message from either an achievement threshold or a random response, and raises the displayed top score when needed. Every tenth press writes the high score to localStorage and asks the achievement store to update the account record; the hundredth awards the `rebel-button` achievement. Higher scores progressively reveal the leaderboard, previous message, AMI mascot, and theme control. Reset clears the local score and session state after confirmation, but it does not call the remote update path. Confetti scales dramatically with the click count. An old sketch, fully animated and very literal about rebellion.

CLICK MEASURE SMILE! The Component compares a logged-in user's click record with the browser's `topScore` on mount and keeps the larger value. A press updates the local count immediately, swaps in achievement or random response text, remembers the previous message, and triggers confetti at achievement thresholds. Every ten clicks persist locally and submit through `achievementStore.updateClickRecord`; a successful update refreshes high-score data. Score gates reveal the leaderboard, last achievement, mascot, and theme toggle. Reset requires a popup confirmation and clears the local score. One caveat under the grin: reset does not submit zero back to the account record. :)

A small bright thing receives the whole stage. The Component reads only the Butterfly store's selected butterfly. When one exists, its rotation and scale become the outer transform, its two wing colors paint four simple wing shapes, and its id, message, rotation, and scale are displayed beneath it. The wings flutter continuously through paired CSS keyframes. When the store has no selection, the card becomes a clear empty state inviting the visitor to choose from the swarm. No selection, editing, capture, or persistence occurs here. It is a companion display: affectionate, focused, and entirely dependent on the selection made elsewhere.

Record of exhibit behavior: `currentButterfly` is a computed projection of `butterflyStore.getSelectedButterfly`. A selected specimen is rendered from CSS shapes rather than an image asset; its top and bottom wing colors, rotation, scale, id, and optional message are read directly from the record. Two 0.3-second keyframe loops animate the left and right wings around separate transform origins. If no record is selected, the Component replaces the specimen with an icon and explanatory empty state. The historical boundary is exact: this view documents the current selection, but does not choose, modify, catch, or save a butterfly.

Two handles carry one interval. Local `minValue` and `maxValue` begin from `modelValue` and are resynchronized whenever that object changes. Each range input uses the supplied outer min, max, and step. Moving the lower value past the upper snaps it back; moving the upper below the lower does the same. Every accepted change emits one `{ min, max }` object. Computed percentages position the colored span, both visual knobs, and hover tooltips, while the label preserves the original range beside the current one. The structure bears its load, although callers should avoid a zero-width outer range because the percentage calculations divide by `max - min`.

Two range inputs share the same trail, friend. The Component keeps local lower and upper values, watches the parent model for replacements, and emits the paired range whenever either side changes. Crossing values are clamped to one another, so the selected interval cannot invert. The fill and knob positions are calculated as percentages of the supplied outer range, and separate hover flags reveal the current values above each knob. Original limits remain visible in the label while current limits lead the line. It is a hand-built dual slider rather than a native two-thumb control, so the visual knob divs themselves do not perform the dragging—the overlaid range inputs do.

The dashboard separates guests from account holders before unpacking the cargo. Guests receive a fallback avatar, login links, a welcome message, and no saved-content panels. Logged-in users receive `user-avatar`, an upload control configured to their user id, theme and jellybean controls, animation selection, profile panel, galleries, cache clearing, card picker, and a sign-out action. The upload target is set on mount and whenever the active user id changes. Logout waits for the user store, then replaces the route with `/login`; failures go to the console. Broad harbor, but mostly composition—the child Components and stores own the actual settings and collections.

The Dashboard answers ‘who is here?’ before ‘what can they manage?’ Guest identity produces the name Kind Guest, a generic icon, login routes, and an intentionally empty saved-content state. A real user produces their username, avatar, upload target, appearance controls, profile panel, galleries, cache control, and card picker. The Component does not fetch these domains itself; it composes their managers and derives only display name, welcome copy, and guest branching. When user identity changes, the upload store is retargeted. Logout ends with a route replacement. Thus the page is less a self than a frame through which several account subsystems become visible.

A simple portrait with sensible leftovers. The effective user id prefers the optional prop and otherwise uses the current user store; the display name follows the same prop-first pattern and falls back to Guest. With no id, the image remains the Kind Art fallback. With an id, `userStore.userImage` resolves the source. The watcher reruns when the effective id changes or when either the active user's or matching cached user's ArtImage id changes, so an avatar update can refresh without rebuilding the parent. A failed image load also swaps the element to the fallback. Nothing here uploads or edits the user—it serves the picture already plated elsewhere.
